title: What is a Receptive Field?
concept: A receptive field is the patch of the original image that can influence one activation in a later layer — not the whole picture, just the upstream region that neuron can 'see'.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Output-size math tells you how large a feature map becomes. What new question does receptive field answer?

options.text: Which part of the original image can influence one activation
How many files the model saves to disk
What color the filter weights should be drawn in diagrams

exercises.explain: Exactly. Output size is about shape; receptive field is about which original pixels a neuron can actually feel.

cards.title: One neuron, one patch
cards.body: Imagine tapping one square in a deep feature map and asking, which part of the original photo could have caused this square to light up? The answer is not usually the whole image. It's a specific upstream patch.

cards.title: What is a receptive field?
cards.body: A receptive field is the region of the original image that can influence one activation in some later layer. If that activation changes, the reason must come from somewhere inside that patch.

cards.title: Stacked windows
cards.body: Picture looking through several windows lined up in a wall. The last window does not magically show you the whole building — it only shows the area that lines up through all the earlier windows. A receptive field is that lined-up visible area.

cards.title: A cat-ear detector
cards.body: Suppose a later-layer activation helps detect a cat's ear. That activation was influenced by the part of the original image containing the ear and nearby pixels — not by a cloud in the opposite corner. The ear-area patch is its receptive field.

cards.title: Not every neuron sees everything
cards.body: A common beginner mistake is to assume that because the full network gets the whole image, every single activation also gets the whole image. Usually it doesn't. Early and middle activations only depend on limited regions unless enough layers have widened their view.

cards.title: Whole Image vs. One Activation's View
cards.body: A deep activation lives inside the full picture, but only part of the picture can affect it:

left.text:
Sky, road, trees,
car, sidewalk —
everything in the photo

right.text:
Only one highlighted patch
of original pixels
can change this value

exercises.prompt: Which description best matches a receptive field?

options.text: The patch of the original image that can affect one activation
The list of all labels the model can predict
The number of channels in the input image
The device screen where the image is displayed

exercises.explain: Right — receptive field means the upstream image region a specific activation can respond to.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Every activation in a CNN automatically depends on every pixel in the original image.
exercises.explain: False — many activations only depend on a limited patch of the image, especially in earlier layers.

exercises.sentence: A receptive field is the region of the original ___ that can influence one activation.
exercises.explain: Image — the question is always about which original pixels could change that activation.

exercises.prompt: Which statement sounds like it came from someone who really understands receptive fields?

options.text: A later-layer neuron responds to a particular patch of the original image, not to every pixel equally
Receptive field just means the brightness of the image
Receptive field is another name for the dataset split

exercises.explain: Exactly — receptive field is about the upstream patch that can influence a specific activation.

exercises.prompt: A deep activation lights up when it sees the edge of a stop sign. Which set of pixels belongs in that activation's receptive field?

options.text: The region around the stop sign edge that could have caused the activation
Every pixel in the full image, no matter how far away
Only the final text label 'stop sign,' because activations never depend on raw pixels
The model's saved weights file on disk

exercises.explain: Exactly. A receptive field is the upstream image patch that can influence one activation. Next, you'll see how that patch grows wider as layers stack on top of each other.
